{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Nara, Japan}} {{About|the World Heritage Site|the shrine in Nagasaki|Kōfuku-ji (Nagasaki)}} {{redirect|Kofuku|the 1981 film|Kofuku (film)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kōfuku-ji | native_name = | image = [[File:Kofukuji12st5s3200.jpg|300px]] | image_size = | alt = | caption = Five-story pagoda and Tōkondō at Kōfuku-ji | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 48 Noboriōji-chō, [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Nara Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34.68325|135.83117|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = [[Hossō]] | deity = [[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyorai]] (Śākyamuni) | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{url|http://www.kohfukuji.com/}} | founded_by = [[Emperor Tenji]] | year_completed = 669 }} {{nihongo|'''Kōfuku-ji'''|興福寺|Kōfuku-ji}} is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[Buddhist temples in Japan|temple]] that was once one of the powerful [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples]] in the city of [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Japan]]. The temple is the national headquarters of the [[Dharma character school|Hossō]] school. == History == [[File:Five story Pagoda of Kohuku-ji (temple) in Nara, Japan (1911 by Elstner Hilton).jpg|left|thumb|Kofuku-ji in 1911]] Kōfuku-ji has its origin as a temple that was established in 669 by Kagami-no-Ōkimi ({{nihongo2|鏡大君}}), the wife of [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]], wishing for her husband's recovery from illness. Its original site was in [[Yamashina-ku, Kyoto|Yamashina]], [[Yamashiro Province]] (present-day [[Kyoto]]). In 672, the temple was moved to [[Fujiwara-kyō]], the first planned Japanese capital to copy the orthogonal grid pattern of [[Chang'an]]. In 710, the temple was dismantled for the second time and moved to its present location, on the east side of the newly constructed capital, [[Heijō-kyō]], today's Nara. Kōfuku-ji was the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]]'s tutelary temple, and enjoyed prosperity for as long as the family did. The temple was not only an important center for the Buddhist religion, but also retained influence over the imperial government, and even by "aggressive means" in some cases.John Bowring, pp.218–219 When many of the [[Nanto Shichi Daiji]], such as [[Tōdai-ji]], declined after the move of capital to Heian-kyō (Kyoto), Kōfuku-ji kept its significance because of its connection to the Fujiwara. The temple was damaged and destroyed by civil wars and fires many times,{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |isbn=1-85409-523-4 |page=200}} and was rebuilt as many times as well, although finally some of the important buildings, such as one of the three golden halls, the Nandaimon, Chūmon and the corridor were never reconstructed and are missing today. The rebuilding of the Central Golden Hall was completed in 2018. {{nihongo|''[[Rokusō-an]]''|六窓庵|Six Window Hut}} was a ''[[chashitsu]]'' formerly located at the temple and considered one of the {{nihongo|''San-meiseki''|三名席| Three Famous Tearooms}}.{{Google books|MorTAgAAQBAJ|Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac|page=163|keywords=San-meiseki|text=|plainurl=}} It was relocated due its deteriorated state and is now in the gardens of the [[Tokyo National Museum]].{{Cite web|url=https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/ondemand/audio/j_art-20150618-1/|title = Rokusoan Teahouse (Rokusoan) – the Magic of Japanese Masterpieces | NHK WORLD-JAPAN on Demand}}{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%AD%E7%AA%93%E5%BA%B5-879272|title = 六窓庵とは}} == Architectures and treasures == [[File:Koufuku-ji Nan'endo 201505a.jpg|thumb|220px|Nan'endō is the No.9 of [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku 33 Pilgrimage]]]] [[File:181029 Chū-kondō of Kōfuku-ji.jpg|thumb|220px|Chū-kondō (Central Golden Hall)]] [[File:Kofukuji09s3200.jpg|thumb|220px|Tō-kondō (East Golden Hall)]] [[File:ASURA Kohfukuji.jpg|thumb|200px|[[Asura (Buddhism)]]]] The following are some of the temple's buildings and treasures of note. === Architecture === * {{nihongo|East Golden Hall|東金堂|Tō-kondō}}, 1425, one of the former three golden halls ([[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2462 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} *{{nihongo|Central Golden Hall|中金堂|Chū-kondō}}, 2018, reconstructed, the former temporary Central Golden Hall building (仮金堂) now serves as the temporary Lecture Hall (仮講堂) * {{nihongo|Five-storied pagoda|五重塔|Gojū-no-tō}}, 1426 (National Treasure){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2461 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|Three-storied pagoda|三重塔|Sanjū-no-tō}}, 1185(National Treasure){{cite web|url=https://www.kohfukuji.com/english/e003/|title=Three-storied Pagoda National Treasure - KOHFUKUJI Temple|lang=en|access-date=9 September 2023}} * {{nihongo|North Octagonal Hall|北円堂|Hoku'endō}}, 1210 (National Treasure){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2459 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|South Octagonal Hall|南円堂|Nan'endō}}, 1741, Site No.9 of [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku 33 Pilgrimage]] ([[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2464 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|Bath House|大湯屋|Ōyūya}}, 1394–1427 (Important Cultural Property){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2463 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} === Treasures === * (Statue) The [[Deva (Buddhism)|Devas]] of the Eight Classes, including dry-lacquer [[Asura (Buddhism)|Ashura]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) [[The ten principal disciples|The Ten Great Disciples]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) [[Avalokiteśvara#Manifestations|Thousand-armed Kannon]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) {{nihongo|[[Avalokiteśvara#Manifestations|Amoghapāśa]]|不空羂索観音|Fukūkensaku Kannon}} attributed to [[Kōkei (sculptor)|Kōkei]], is housed in Nan'endō (National Treasure) == Plan == Showing the original layout of the temple, with the later three-storied pagoda, Nan'en-dō, and Ōyūya superimposed. Of the buildings marked, only these three together with the five-storied pagoda, Tōkon-dō and Hoku'en-dō remain.{{cite book |title=Early Buddhist Architecture in Japan |author=Suzuki, Kakichi |publisher=[[Kodansha]] |year=1980 |page=86 |isbn=0-87011-386-0}} {| width="415px" align="center" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0" |- | align=center|

oldest sculpture made by the ''Busshi'' [[Kaikei]], part of the temple's collection until 1906.{{Cite web |date=2021-05-30 |title=Miroku, the Bodhisattva of the Future – Works – Museum of Fine Arts, Boston |url=https://collections.mfa.org/objects/28592 |access-date=2024-02-17 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210530053520/https://collections.mfa.org/objects/28592 |archive-date=2021-05-30 }} *[[Burke Jizō]] - sculpture by Kaikei, now a part of the [[Metropolitan Museum of Art]] *[[Statue of Jizō (Intan)]] - another sculpture of Jizō, part of the MET. === Works of art from Kōfuku-ji === * ''[[The Final Death of the Buddha Sakyamuni]]'' - [[Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō]] painting depicting [[Parinirvana]] (1320-1340) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * {{cite book | last = John Bowring | first = Richard | title = The religious traditions of Japan | publisher = Cambridge University Press | year = 2005 | pages = 77–78 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=GzeODCVG26UC | isbn = 0-521-85119-X }} * {{cite book | last = Noma | first = Seiroku |author2=John M. Rosenfield |author3=Bin Takahashi | title = The Arts of Japan: Ancient and medieval | publisher = Kodansha International | year = 2003 | pages = 84–85 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=RfswGk-C0ykC | isbn = 4-7700-2977-2 }} == External links == {{Commons category|Kōfuku-ji}} * [http://www.kohfukuji.com/english.html Kōfuku-ji web site] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191229071912/http://www.kohfukuji.com/english.html |date=2019-12-29 }} * [http://www.kohfukuji.com/ Kōfuku-ji web site]{{in lang|ja}} * {{nihongo|''San-meiseki''|三名席| Three Famous Tearooms}}.{{Google books|MorTAgAAQBAJ|Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac|page=163|keywords=San-meiseki|text=|plainurl=}} It was relocated due its deteriorated state and is now in the gardens of the [[Tokyo National Museum]].{{Cite web|url=https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/ondemand/audio/j_art-20150618-1/|title = Rokusoan {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Nara, Japan}} {{About|the World Heritage Site|the shrine in Nagasaki|Kōfuku-ji (Nagasaki)}} {{redirect|Kofuku|the 1981 film|Kofuku (film)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kōfuku-ji | native_name = | image = [[File:Kofukuji12st5s3200.jpg|300px]] | image_size = | alt = | caption = Five-story pagoda and Tōkondō at Kōfuku-ji | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 48 Noboriōji-chō, [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Nara Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34.68325|135.83117|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = [[Hossō]] | deity = [[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyorai]] (Śākyamuni) | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{url|http://www.kohfukuji.com/}} | founded_by = [[Emperor Tenji]] | year_completed = 669 }} {{nihongo|'''Kōfuku-ji'''|興福寺|Kōfuku-ji}} is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[Buddhist temples in Japan|temple]] that was once one of the powerful [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples]] in the city of [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Japan]]. The temple is the national headquarters of the [[Dharma character school|Hossō]] school. == History == [[File:Five story Pagoda of Kohuku-ji (temple) in Nara, Japan (1911 by Elstner Hilton).jpg|left|thumb|Kofuku-ji in 1911]] Kōfuku-ji has its origin as a temple that was established in 669 by Kagami-no-Ōkimi ({{nihongo2|鏡大君}}), the wife of [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]], wishing for her husband's recovery from illness. Its original site was in [[Yamashina-ku, Kyoto|Yamashina]], [[Yamashiro Province]] (present-day [[Kyoto]]). In 672, the temple was moved to [[Fujiwara-kyō]], the first planned Japanese capital to copy the orthogonal grid pattern of [[Chang'an]]. In 710, the temple was dismantled for the second time and moved to its present location, on the east side of the newly constructed capital, [[Heijō-kyō]], today's Nara. Kōfuku-ji was the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]]'s tutelary temple, and enjoyed prosperity for as long as the family did. The temple was not only an important center for the Buddhist religion, but also retained influence over the imperial government, and even by "aggressive means" in some cases.John Bowring, pp.218–219 When many of the [[Nanto Shichi Daiji]], such as [[Tōdai-ji]], declined after the move of capital to Heian-kyō (Kyoto), Kōfuku-ji kept its significance because of its connection to the Fujiwara. The temple was damaged and destroyed by civil wars and fires many times,{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |isbn=1-85409-523-4 |page=200}} and was rebuilt as many times as well, although finally some of the important buildings, such as one of the three golden halls, the Nandaimon, Chūmon and the corridor were never reconstructed and are missing today. The rebuilding of the Central Golden Hall was completed in 2018. {{nihongo|''[[Rokusō-an]]''|六窓庵|Six Window Hut}} was a ''[[chashitsu]]'' formerly located at the temple and considered one of the {{nihongo|''San-meiseki''|三名席| Three Famous Tearooms}}.{{Google books|MorTAgAAQBAJ|Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac|page=163|keywords=San-meiseki|text=|plainurl=}} It was relocated due its deteriorated state and is now in the gardens of the [[Tokyo National Museum]].{{Cite web|url=https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/ondemand/audio/j_art-20150618-1/|title = Rokusoan Teahouse (Rokusoan) – the Magic of Japanese Masterpieces | NHK WORLD-JAPAN on Demand}}{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%AD%E7%AA%93%E5%BA%B5-879272|title = 六窓庵とは}} == Architectures and treasures == [[File:Koufuku-ji Nan'endo 201505a.jpg|thumb|220px|Nan'endō is the No.9 of [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku 33 Pilgrimage]]]] [[File:181029 Chū-kondō of Kōfuku-ji.jpg|thumb|220px|Chū-kondō (Central Golden Hall)]] [[File:Kofukuji09s3200.jpg|thumb|220px|Tō-kondō (East Golden Hall)]] [[File:ASURA Kohfukuji.jpg|thumb|200px|[[Asura (Buddhism)]]]] The following are some of the temple's buildings and treasures of note. === Architecture === * {{nihongo|East Golden Hall|東金堂|Tō-kondō}}, 1425, one of the former three golden halls ([[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2462 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} *{{nihongo|Central Golden Hall|中金堂|Chū-kondō}}, 2018, reconstructed, the former temporary Central Golden Hall building (仮金堂) now serves as the temporary Lecture Hall (仮講堂) * {{nihongo|Five-storied pagoda|五重塔|Gojū-no-tō}}, 1426 (National Treasure){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2461 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|Three-storied pagoda|三重塔|Sanjū-no-tō}}, 1185(National Treasure){{cite web|url=https://www.kohfukuji.com/english/e003/|title=Three-storied Pagoda National Treasure - KOHFUKUJI Temple|lang=en|access-date=9 September 2023}} * {{nihongo|North Octagonal Hall|北円堂|Hoku'endō}}, 1210 (National Treasure){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2459 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|South Octagonal Hall|南円堂|Nan'endō}}, 1741, Site No.9 of [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku 33 Pilgrimage]] ([[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2464 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|Bath House|大湯屋|Ōyūya}}, 1394–1427 (Important Cultural Property){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2463 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} === Treasures === * (Statue) The [[Deva (Buddhism)|Devas]] of the Eight Classes, including dry-lacquer [[Asura (Buddhism)|Ashura]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) [[The ten principal disciples|The Ten Great Disciples]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) [[Avalokiteśvara#Manifestations|Thousand-armed Kannon]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) {{nihongo|[[Avalokiteśvara#Manifestations|Amoghapāśa]]|不空羂索観音|Fukūkensaku Kannon}} attributed to [[Kōkei (sculptor)|Kōkei]], is housed in Nan'endō (National Treasure) == Plan == Showing the original layout of the temple, with the later three-storied pagoda, Nan'en-dō, and Ōyūya superimposed. Of the buildings marked, only these three together with the five-storied pagoda, Tōkon-dō and Hoku'en-dō remain.{{cite book |title=Early Buddhist Architecture in Japan |author=Suzuki, Kakichi |publisher=[[Kodansha]] |year=1980 |page=86 |isbn=0-87011-386-0}} {| width="415px" align="center" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0" |- | align=center|

oldest sculpture made by the ''Busshi'' [[Kaikei]], part of the temple's collection until 1906.{{Cite web |date=2021-05-30 |title=Miroku, the Bodhisattva of the Future – Works – Museum of Fine Arts, Boston |url=https://collections.mfa.org/objects/28592 |access-date=2024-02-17 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210530053520/https://collections.mfa.org/objects/28592 |archive-date=2021-05-30 }} *[[Burke Jizō]] - sculpture by Kaikei, now a part of the [[Metropolitan Museum of Art]] *[[Statue of Jizō (Intan)]] - another sculpture of Jizō, part of the MET. === Works of art from Kōfuku-ji === * ''[[The Final Death of the Buddha Sakyamuni]]'' - [[Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō]] painting depicting [[Parinirvana]] (1320-1340) == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * {{cite book | last = John Bowring | first = Richard | title = The religious traditions of Japan | publisher = Cambridge University Press | year = 2005 | pages = 77–78 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=GzeODCVG26UC | isbn = 0-521-85119-X }} * {{cite book | last = Noma | first = Seiroku |author2=John M. Rosenfield |author3=Bin Takahashi | title = The Arts of Japan: Ancient and medieval | publisher = Kodansha International | year = 2003 | pages = 84–85 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=RfswGk-C0ykC | isbn = 4-7700-2977-2 }} == External links == {{Commons category|Kōfuku-ji}} * [http://www.kohfukuji.com/english.html Kōfuku-ji web site] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191229071912/http://www.kohfukuji.com/english.html |date=2019-12-29 }} * [http://www.kohfukuji.com/ Kōfuku-ji web site]{{in lang|ja}} * The temple was damaged and destroyed by civil wars and fires many times,{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |isbn=1-85409-523-4 |page=200}} and was rebuilt as many times as well, although finally some of the important buildings, such as one of the three golden halls, the Nandaimon, Chūmon and the corridor were never reconstructed and are missing today. The rebuilding of the Central Golden Hall was completed in 2018. {{nihongo|''[[Rokusō-an]]''|六窓庵|Six Window Hut}} was a ''[[chashitsu]]'' formerly located at the temple and considered one of the {{nihongo|''San-meiseki''|三名席| Three Famous Tearooms}}.{{Google books|MorTAgAAQBAJ|Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac|page=163|keywords=San-meiseki|text=|plainurl=}} It was relocated due its deteriorated state and is now in the gardens of the [[Tokyo National Museum]].{{Cite web|url=https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/ondemand/audio/j_art-20150618-1/|title = Rokusoan Teahouse (Rokusoan) – the Magic of Japanese Masterpieces | NHK WORLD-JAPAN on Demand}}{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%AD%E7%AA%93%E5%BA%B5-879272|title = 六窓庵とは}} == Architectures and treasures == [[File:Koufuku-ji Nan'endo 201505a.jpg|thumb|220px|Nan'endō is the No.9 of [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku 33 Pilgrimage]]]] [[File:181029 Chū-kondō of of Japan|National Treasure]]){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2462 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} *{{nihongo|Central Golden Hall|中金堂|Chū-kondō}}, 2018, reconstructed, the former temporary Central Golden Hall building (仮金堂) now serves as the temporary Lecture Hall (仮講堂) * {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Nara, Japan}} {{About|the World Heritage Site|the shrine in Nagasaki|Kōfuku-ji (Nagasaki)}} {{redirect|Kofuku|the 1981 film|Kofuku (film)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kōfuku-ji | native_name = | image = [[File:Kofukuji12st5s3200.jpg|300px]] | image_size = | alt = | caption = Five-story pagoda and Tōkondō at Kōfuku-ji | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 48 Noboriōji-chō, [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Nara Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34.68325|135.83117|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = [[Hossō]] | deity = [[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyorai]] (Śākyamuni) | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{url|http://www.kohfukuji.com/}} | founded_by = [[Emperor Tenji]] | year_completed = 669 }} {{nihongo|'''Kōfuku-ji'''|興福寺|Kōfuku-ji}} is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[Buddhist temples in Japan|temple]] that was once one of the powerful [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples]] in the city of [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Japan]]. The temple is the national headquarters of the [[Dharma character school|Hossō]] school. == History == [[File:Five story Pagoda of Kohuku-ji (temple) in Nara, Japan (1911 by Elstner Hilton).jpg|left|thumb|Kofuku-ji in 1911]] Kōfuku-ji has its origin as a temple that was established in 669 by Kagami-no-Ōkimi ({{nihongo2|鏡大君}}), the wife of [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]], wishing for her husband's recovery from illness. Its original site was in [[Yamashina-ku, Kyoto|Yamashina]], [[Yamashiro Province]] (present-day [[Kyoto]]). In 672, the temple was moved to [[Fujiwara-kyō]], the first planned Japanese capital to copy the orthogonal grid pattern of [[Chang'an]]. In 710, the temple was dismantled for the second time and moved to its present location, on the east side of the newly constructed capital, [[Heijō-kyō]], today's Nara. Kōfuku-ji was the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]]'s tutelary temple, and enjoyed prosperity for as long as the family did. The temple was not only an important center for the Buddhist religion, but also retained influence over the imperial government, and even by "aggressive means" in some cases.John Bowring, pp.218–219 When many of the [[Nanto Shichi Daiji]], such as [[Tōdai-ji]], declined after the move of capital to Heian-kyō (Kyoto), Kōfuku-ji kept its significance because of its connection to the Fujiwara. The temple was damaged and destroyed by civil wars and fires many times,{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |isbn=1-85409-523-4 |page=200}} and was rebuilt as many times as well, although finally some of the important buildings, such as one of the three golden halls, the Nandaimon, Chūmon and the corridor were never reconstructed and are missing today. The rebuilding of the Central Golden Hall was completed in 2018. {{nihongo|''[[Rokusō-an]]''|六窓庵|Six Window Hut}} was a ''[[chashitsu]]'' formerly located at the temple and considered one of the {{nihongo|''San-meiseki''|三名席| Three Famous Tearooms}}.{{Google books|MorTAgAAQBAJ|Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac|page=163|keywords=San-meiseki|text=|plainurl=}} It was relocated due its deteriorated state and is now in the gardens of the [[Tokyo National Museum]].{{Cite web|url=https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/ondemand/audio/j_art-20150618-1/|title = Rokusoan Teahouse (Rokusoan) – the Magic of Japanese Masterpieces | NHK WORLD-JAPAN on Demand}}{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%AD%E7%AA%93%E5%BA%B5-879272|title = 六窓庵とは}} == Architectures and treasures == [[File:Koufuku-ji Nan'endo 201505a.jpg|thumb|220px|Nan'endō is the No.9 of [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku 33 Pilgrimage]]]] [[File:181029 Chū-kondō of Kōfuku-ji.jpg|thumb|220px|Chū-kondō (Central Golden Hall)]] [[File:Kofukuji09s3200.jpg|thumb|220px|Tō-kondō (East Golden Hall)]] [[File:ASURA Kohfukuji.jpg|thumb|200px|[[Asura (Buddhism)]]]] The following are some of the temple's buildings and treasures of note. === Architecture === * {{nihongo|East Golden Hall|東金堂|Tō-kondō}}, 1425, one of the former three golden halls ([[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2462 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} *{{nihongo|Central Golden Hall|中金堂|Chū-kondō}}, 2018, reconstructed, the former temporary Central Golden Hall building (仮金堂) now serves as the temporary Lecture Hall (仮講堂) * {{nihongo|Five-storied pagoda|五重塔|Gojū-no-tō}}, 1426 (National Treasure){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2461 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|Three-storied pagoda|三重塔|Sanjū-no-tō}}, 1185(National Treasure){{cite web|url=https://www.kohfukuji.com/english/e003/|title=Three-storied Pagoda National Treasure - KOHFUKUJI Temple|lang=en|access-date=9 September 2023}} * {{nihongo|North Octagonal Hall|北円堂|Hoku'endō}}, 1210 (National Treasure){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2459 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|South Octagonal Hall|南円堂|Nan'endō}}, 1741, Site No.9 of [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku 33 Pilgrimage]] ([[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2464 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|Bath House|大湯屋|Ōyūya}}, 1394–1427 (Important Cultural Property){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2463 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} === Treasures === * (Statue) The [[Deva (Buddhism)|Devas]] of the Eight Classes, including dry-lacquer [[Asura (Buddhism)|Ashura]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) [[The ten principal disciples|The Ten Great Disciples]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) [[Avalokiteśvara#Manifestations|Thousand-armed Kannon]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) {{nihongo|[[Avalokiteśvara#Manifestations|Amoghapāśa]]|不空羂索観音|Fukūkensaku Kannon}} attributed to [[Kōkei (sculptor)|Kōkei]], is housed in Nan'endō (National Treasure) == Plan == Showing the original layout of the temple, with the later three-storied pagoda, Nan'en-dō, and Ōyūya superimposed. Of the buildings marked, only these three together with the five-storied pagoda, Tōkon-dō and Hoku'en-dō remain.{{cite book |title=Early Buddhist Architecture in Japan |author=Suzuki, Kakichi |publisher=[[Kodansha]] |year=1980 |page=86 |isbn=0-87011-386-0}} {| width="415px" align="center" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0" |- | align=center|

oldest sculpture made by the ''Busshi'' [[Kaikei]], part of the temple's collection until 1906.{{Cite web |date=2021-05-30 |title=Miroku, the Bodhisattva of the Future – Works – Museum of Fine Arts, Boston |url=https://collections.mfa.org/objects/28592 |access-date=2024-02-17 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210530053520/https://collections.mfa.org/objects/28592 |archive-date=2021-05-30 }} *[[Burke Jizō]] - {{nihongo|''San-meiseki''|三名席| Three Famous Tearooms}}.{{Google books|MorTAgAAQBAJ|Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac|page=163|keywords=San-meiseki|text=|plainurl=}} It was {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Nara, Japan}} {{About|the World Heritage Site|the shrine in Nagasaki|Kōfuku-ji (Nagasaki)}} {{redirect|Kofuku|the 1981 film|Kofuku (film)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kōfuku-ji | native_name = | image = [[File:Kofukuji12st5s3200.jpg|300px]] | image_size = | alt = | caption = Five-story pagoda and Tōkondō at Kōfuku-ji | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 48 Noboriōji-chō, [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Nara Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34.68325|135.83117|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = [[Hossō]] | deity = [[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyorai]] (Śākyamuni) | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{url|http://www.kohfukuji.com/}} | founded_by = [[Emperor Tenji]] | year_completed = 669 }} {{nihongo|'''Kōfuku-ji'''|興福寺|Kōfuku-ji}} is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[Buddhist temples in Japan|temple]] that was once one of the powerful [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples]] in the city of [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Japan]]. The temple is the national headquarters of the [[Dharma character school|Hossō]] school. == History == [[File:Five story Pagoda of Kohuku-ji (temple) in Nara, Japan (1911 by Elstner Hilton).jpg|left|thumb|Kofuku-ji in 1911]] Kōfuku-ji has its origin as a temple that was established in 669 by Kagami-no-Ōkimi ({{nihongo2|鏡大君}}), the wife of [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]], wishing for her husband's recovery from illness. Its original site was in [[Yamashina-ku, Kyoto|Yamashina]], [[Yamashiro Province]] (present-day [[Kyoto]]). In 672, the temple was moved to [[Fujiwara-kyō]], the first planned Japanese capital to copy the orthogonal grid pattern of [[Chang'an]]. In 710, the temple was dismantled for the second time and moved to its present location, on the east side of the newly constructed capital, [[Heijō-kyō]], today's Nara. Kōfuku-ji was the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]]'s tutelary temple, and enjoyed prosperity for as long as the family did. The temple was not only an important center for the Buddhist religion, but also retained influence over the imperial government, and even by "aggressive means" in some cases.John Bowring, pp.218–219 When many of the [[Nanto Shichi Daiji]], such as [[Tōdai-ji]], declined after the move of capital to Heian-kyō (Kyoto), Kōfuku-ji kept its significance because of its connection to the Fujiwara. The temple was damaged and destroyed by civil wars and fires many times,{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |isbn=1-85409-523-4 |page=200}} and was rebuilt as many times as well, although finally some of the important buildings, such as one of the three golden halls, the Nandaimon, Chūmon and the corridor were never reconstructed and are missing today. The rebuilding of the Central Golden Hall was completed in 2018. {{nihongo|''[[Rokusō-an]]''|六窓庵|Six Window Hut}} was a ''[[chashitsu]]'' formerly located at the temple and considered one of the {{nihongo|''San-meiseki''|三名席| Three Famous Tearooms}}.{{Google books|MorTAgAAQBAJ|Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac|page=163|keywords=San-meiseki|text=|plainurl=}} It was relocated due its deteriorated state and is now in the gardens of the [[Tokyo National Museum]].{{Cite web|url=https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/ondemand/audio/j_art-20150618-1/|title = Rokusoan Teahouse (Rokusoan) – the Magic of Japanese Masterpieces | NHK WORLD-JAPAN on Demand}}{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%AD%E7%AA%93%E5%BA%B5-879272|title = 六窓庵とは}} == Architectures and treasures == [[File:Koufuku-ji Nan'endo 201505a.jpg|thumb|220px|Nan'endō is the No.9 of [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku 33 Pilgrimage]]]] [[File:181029 Chū-kondō of Kōfuku-ji.jpg|thumb|220px|Chū-kondō (Central Golden Hall)]] [[File:Kofukuji09s3200.jpg|thumb|220px|Tō-kondō (East Golden Hall)]] [[File:ASURA Kohfukuji.jpg|thumb|200px|[[Asura (Buddhism)]]]] The following are some of the temple's buildings and treasures of note. === Architecture === * {{nihongo|East Golden Hall|東金堂|Tō-kondō}}, 1425, one of the former three golden halls ([[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2462 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} *{{nihongo|Central Golden Hall|中金堂|Chū-kondō}}, 2018, reconstructed, the former temporary Central Golden Hall building (仮金堂) now serves as the temporary Lecture Hall (仮講堂) * {{nihongo|Five-storied pagoda|五重塔|Gojū-no-tō}}, 1426 (National Treasure){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2461 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|Three-storied pagoda|三重塔|Sanjū-no-tō}}, 1185(National Treasure){{cite web|url=https://www.kohfukuji.com/english/e003/|title=Three-storied Pagoda National Treasure - KOHFUKUJI Temple|lang=en|access-date=9 September 2023}} * {{nihongo|North Octagonal Hall|北円堂|Hoku'endō}}, 1210 (National Treasure){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2459 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|South Octagonal Hall|南円堂|Nan'endō}}, 1741, Site No.9 of [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku 33 Pilgrimage]] ([[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2464 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|Bath House|大湯屋|Ōyūya}}, 1394–1427 (Important Cultural Property){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2463 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} === Treasures === * (Statue) The [[Deva (Buddhism)|Devas]] of the Eight Classes, including dry-lacquer [[Asura (Buddhism)|Ashura]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) [[The ten principal disciples|The Ten Great Disciples]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) [[Avalokiteśvara#Manifestations|Thousand-armed Kannon]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) {{nihongo|[[Avalokiteśvara#Manifestations|Amoghapāśa]]|不空羂索観音|Fukūkensaku Kannon}} attributed to [[Kōkei (sculptor)|Kōkei]], is housed in Nan'endō (National Treasure) == Plan == Showing the original layout of the temple, with the later three-storied pagoda, Nan'en-dō, and Ōyūya superimposed. Of the buildings marked, only these three together with the five-storied pagoda, Tōkon-dō and Hoku'en-dō remain.{{cite book |title=Early Buddhist Architecture in Japan |author=Suzuki, Kakichi |publisher=[[Kodansha]] |year=1980 |page=86 |isbn=0-87011-386-0}} {| width="415px" align="center" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0" |- | align=center|

The temple was not only an important center for the Buddhist religion, but also retained influence over the imperial government, and even by "aggressive means" in some cases.John Bowring, pp.218–219 When many of the [[Nanto Shichi Daiji]], such as [[Tōdai-ji]], declined after the move of capital to Heian-kyō (Kyoto), Kōfuku-ji kept its significance because of its connection to the Fujiwara. The temple was damaged and destroyed by civil wars and fires many times,{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |isbn=1-85409-523-4 |page=200}} and was rebuilt as many times as well, although finally some of the important buildings, such as one of the three golden halls, the Nandaimon, Chūmon and the corridor were never reconstructed and are missing today. The rebuilding of the Central Golden Hall was completed in 2018. {{nihongo|''[[Rokusō-an]]''|六窓庵|Six Window Hut}} was a ''[[chashitsu]]'' formerly located at the temple and considered one of the {{nihongo|''San-meiseki''|三名席| Three Famous Tearooms}}.{{Google books|MorTAgAAQBAJ|Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac|page=163|keywords=San-meiseki|text=|plainurl=}} It was relocated due its deteriorated state and is now in the gardens of the [[Tokyo National Museum]].{{Cite web|url=https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/ondemand/audio/j_art-20150618-1/|title = Rokusoan Teahouse (Rokusoan) – the Magic of Japanese Masterpieces | NHK WORLD-JAPAN on Demand}}{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%AD%E7%AA%93%E5%BA%B5-879272|title = 六窓庵とは}} == Architectures and treasures == [[File:Koufuku-ji Nan'endo 201505a.jpg|thumb|220px|Nan'endō is the No.9 of [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku 33 Pilgrimage]]]] [[File:181029 Chū-kondō of Kōfuku-ji.jpg|thumb|220px|Chū-kondō (Central Golden Hall)]] [[File:Kofukuji09s3200.jpg|thumb|220px|Tō-kondō (East Golden Hall)]] [[File:ASURA Kohfukuji.jpg|thumb|200px|[[Asura (Buddhism)]]]] The following are some of the temple's buildings and treasures of note. === Architecture === * {{nihongo|East Golden Hall|東金堂|Tō-kondō}}, 1425, one of the former three golden halls ([[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2462 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} *{{nihongo|Central Golden Hall|中金堂|Chū-kondō}}, 2018, reconstructed, the former temporary Central Golden Hall building (仮金堂) now serves as the temporary Lecture Hall (仮講堂) * {{nihongo|Five-storied pagoda|五重塔|Gojū-no-tō}}, 1426 (National Treasure){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2461 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|Three-storied pagoda|三重塔|Sanjū-no-tō}}, 1185(National Treasure){{cite web|url=https://www.kohfukuji.com/english/e003/|title=Three-storied Pagoda National Treasure - KOHFUKUJI Temple|lang=en|access-date=9 September 2023}} * {{nihongo|North Octagonal Hall|北円堂|Hoku'endō}}, 1210 (National Treasure){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2459 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|South Octagonal Hall|南円堂|Nan'endō}}, 1741, Site No.9 of [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku 33 Pilgrimage]] ([[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2464 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|Bath House|大湯屋|Ōyūya}}, 1394–1427 (Important Cultural Property){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2463 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} === Treasures === * (Statue) The [[Deva (Buddhism)|Devas]] of the Eight Classes, including dry-lacquer [[Asura (Buddhism)|Ashura]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) [[The ten principal disciples|The Ten Great Disciples]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) [[Avalokiteśvara#Manifestations|Thousand-armed Kannon]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) {{nihongo|[[Avalokiteśvara#Manifestations|Amoghapāśa]]|不空羂索観音|Fukūkensaku Kannon}} attributed to [[Kōkei (sculptor)|Kōkei]], is housed in Nan'endō (National Treasure) == Of the buildings marked, only these three together with the five-storied pagoda, Tōkon-dō and Hoku'en-dō remain.{{cite book |title=Early Buddhist Architecture in Japan |author=Suzuki, Kakichi |publisher=[[Kodansha]] |year=1980 |page=86 |isbn=0-87011-386-0}} {| width="415px" align="center" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0" |- | align=center|

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Nara, Japan}} {{About|the World Heritage Site|the shrine in Nagasaki|Kōfuku-ji (Nagasaki)}} {{redirect|Kofuku|the 1981 film|Kofuku (film)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kōfuku-ji | native_name = | image = [[File:Kofukuji12st5s3200.jpg|300px]] | image_size = | alt = | caption = Five-story pagoda and Tōkondō at Kōfuku-ji | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 48 Noboriōji-chō, [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Nara Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34.68325|135.83117|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = [[Hossō]] | deity = [[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyorai]] (Śākyamuni) | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{url|http://www.kohfukuji.com/}} | founded_by = [[Emperor Tenji]] | year_completed = 669 }} {{nihongo|'''Kōfuku-ji'''|興福寺|Kōfuku-ji}} is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[Buddhist temples in Japan|temple]] that was once one of the powerful [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples]] in the city of [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Japan]]. The temple is the national headquarters of the [[Dharma character school|Hossō]] school. == History == [[File:Five story Pagoda of Kohuku-ji (temple) in Nara, Japan (1911 by Elstner Hilton).jpg|left|thumb|Kofuku-ji in 1911]] Kōfuku-ji has its origin as a temple that was established in 669 by Kagami-no-Ōkimi ({{nihongo2|鏡大君}}), the wife of [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]], wishing for her husband's recovery from illness. Its original site was in [[Yamashina-ku, Kyoto|Yamashina]], [[Yamashiro Province]] (present-day [[Kyoto]]). In 672, the temple was moved to [[Fujiwara-kyō]], the first planned Japanese capital to copy the orthogonal grid pattern of [[Chang'an]]. In 710, the temple was dismantled for the second time and moved to its present location, on the east side of the newly constructed capital, [[Heijō-kyō]], today's Nara. Kōfuku-ji was the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]]'s tutelary temple, and enjoyed prosperity for as long as the family did. The temple was not only an important center for the Buddhist religion, but also retained influence over the imperial government, and even by "aggressive means" in some cases.John Bowring, pp.218–219 When many of the [[Nanto Shichi Daiji]], such as [[Tōdai-ji]], declined after the move of capital to Heian-kyō (Kyoto), Kōfuku-ji kept its significance because of its connection to the Fujiwara. The temple was damaged and destroyed by civil wars and fires many times,{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |isbn=1-85409-523-4 |page=200}} and was rebuilt as many times as well, although finally some of the important buildings, such as one of the three golden halls, the Nandaimon, Chūmon and the corridor were never reconstructed and are missing today. The rebuilding of the Central Golden Hall was completed in 2018. {{nihongo|''[[Rokusō-an]]''|六窓庵|Six Window Hut}} was a ''[[chashitsu]]'' formerly located at the temple and considered one of the {{nihongo|''San-meiseki''|三名席| Three Famous Tearooms}}.{{Google books|MorTAgAAQBAJ|Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac|page=163|keywords=San-meiseki|text=|plainurl=}} It was relocated due its deteriorated state and is now in the gardens of the [[Tokyo National Museum]].{{Cite web|url=https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/ondemand/audio/j_art-20150618-1/|title = Rokusoan Teahouse (Rokusoan) – the Magic of Japanese Masterpieces | NHK WORLD-JAPAN on Demand}}{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%AD%E7%AA%93%E5%BA%B5-879272|title = 六窓庵とは}} == Architectures and treasures == [[File:Koufuku-ji Nan'endo 201505a.jpg|thumb|220px|Nan'endō is the No.9 of [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku 33 Pilgrimage]]]] [[File:181029 Chū-kondō of Kōfuku-ji.jpg|thumb|220px|Chū-kondō (Central Golden Hall)]] [[File:Kofukuji09s3200.jpg|thumb|220px|Tō-kondō (East Golden Hall)]] [[File:ASURA Kohfukuji.jpg|thumb|200px|[[Asura (Buddhism)]]]] The following are some of the temple's buildings and treasures of note. === Architecture === * {{nihongo|East Golden Hall|東金堂|Tō-kondō}}, 1425, one of the former three golden halls ([[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2462 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} *{{nihongo|Central Golden Hall|中金堂|Chū-kondō}}, 2018, reconstructed, the former temporary Central Golden Hall building (仮金堂) now serves as the temporary Lecture Hall (仮講堂) * {{nihongo|Five-storied pagoda|五重塔|Gojū-no-tō}}, 1426 (National Treasure){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2461 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|Three-storied pagoda|三重塔|Sanjū-no-tō}}, 1185(National Treasure){{cite web|url=https://www.kohfukuji.com/english/e003/|title=Three-storied Pagoda National Treasure - KOHFUKUJI Temple|lang=en|access-date=9 September 2023}} * {{nihongo|North Octagonal Hall|北円堂|Hoku'endō}}, 1210 (National Treasure){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2459 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|South Octagonal Hall|南円堂|Nan'endō}}, 1741, Site No.9 of [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku 33 Pilgrimage]] ([[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2464 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|Bath House|大湯屋|Ōyūya}}, 1394–1427 (Important Cultural Property){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2463 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} === Treasures === * (Statue) The [[Deva (Buddhism)|Devas]] of the Eight Classes, including dry-lacquer [[Asura (Buddhism)|Ashura]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) [[The ten principal disciples|The Ten Great Disciples]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) [[Avalokiteśvara#Manifestations|Thousand-armed Kannon]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) {{nihongo|[[Avalokiteśvara#Manifestations|Amoghapāśa]]|不空羂索観音|Fukūkensaku Kannon}} attributed to [[Kōkei (sculptor)|Kōkei]], is housed in Nan'endō (National Treasure) == Plan == Showing the original layout of the temple, with the later three-storied pagoda, Nan'en-dō, and Ōyūya superimposed. Of the buildings marked, only these three together with the five-storied pagoda, Tōkon-dō and Hoku'en-dō remain.{{cite book |title=Early Buddhist Architecture in Japan |author=Suzuki, Kakichi |publisher=[[Kodansha]] |year=1980 |page=86 |isbn=0-87011-386-0}} {| width="415px" align="center" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0" |- | align=center|
[[File:Kōfukuji plan.png|400px]]

{{nihongo|''San-meiseki''|三名席| Three Famous Tearooms}}.{{Google books|MorTAgAAQBAJ|Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac|page=163|keywords=San-meiseki|text=|plainurl=}} It was relocated due its deteriorated state and is now in the gardens of the [[Tokyo National Museum]].{{Cite web|url=https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/ondemand/audio/j_art-20150618-1/|title = Rokusoan Teahouse (Rokusoan) – the Magic of Japanese Masterpieces | NHK WORLD-JAPAN on Demand}}{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%AD%E7%AA%93%E5%BA%B5-879272|title = 六窓庵とは}} == Architectures and (National Treasure) * (Statue) {{nihongo|[[Avalokiteśvara#Manifestations|Amoghapāśa]]|不空羂索観音|Fukūkensaku Kannon}} attributed to [[Kōkei (sculptor)|Kōkei]], is housed in Nan'endō (National Treasure) == Of the buildings marked, only these three together with the five-storied pagoda, Tōkon-dō and Hoku'en-dō remain.{{cite book |title=Early Buddhist Architecture in Japan |author=Suzuki, Kakichi |publisher=[[Kodansha]] |year=1980 |page=86 |isbn=0-87011-386-0}} {| width="415px" align="center" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0" |- | align=center|

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Nara, Japan}} {{About|the World Heritage Site|the shrine in Nagasaki|Kōfuku-ji (Nagasaki)}} {{redirect|Kofuku|the 1981 film|Kofuku (film)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kōfuku-ji | native_name = | image = [[File:Kofukuji12st5s3200.jpg|300px]] | image_size = | alt = | caption = Five-story pagoda and Tōkondō at Kōfuku-ji | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 48 Noboriōji-chō, [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Nara Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34.68325|135.83117|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = [[Hossō]] | deity = [[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyorai]] (Śākyamuni) | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{url|http://www.kohfukuji.com/}} | founded_by = [[Emperor Tenji]] | year_completed = 669 }} {{nihongo|'''Kōfuku-ji'''|興福寺|Kōfuku-ji}} is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[Buddhist temples in Japan|temple]] that was once one of the powerful [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples]] in the city of [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Japan]]. The temple is the national headquarters of the [[Dharma character school|Hossō]] school. == History == [[File:Five story Pagoda of Kohuku-ji (temple) in Nara, Japan (1911 by Elstner Hilton).jpg|left|thumb|Kofuku-ji in 1911]] Kōfuku-ji has its origin as a temple that was established in 669 by Kagami-no-Ōkimi ({{nihongo2|鏡大君}}), the wife of [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]], wishing for her husband's recovery from illness. Its original site was in [[Yamashina-ku, Kyoto|Yamashina]], [[Yamashiro Province]] (present-day [[Kyoto]]). In 672, the temple was moved to [[Fujiwara-kyō]], the first planned Japanese capital to copy the orthogonal grid pattern of [[Chang'an]]. In 710, the temple was dismantled for the second time and moved to its present location, on the east side of the newly constructed capital, [[Heijō-kyō]], today's Nara. Kōfuku-ji was the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]]'s tutelary temple, and enjoyed prosperity for as long as the family did. The temple was not only an important center for the Buddhist religion, but also retained influence over the imperial government, and even by "aggressive means" in some cases.John Bowring, pp.218–219 When many of the [[Nanto Shichi Daiji]], such as [[Tōdai-ji]], declined after the move of capital to Heian-kyō (Kyoto), Kōfuku-ji kept its significance because of its connection to the Fujiwara. The temple was damaged and destroyed by civil wars and fires many times,{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |isbn=1-85409-523-4 |page=200}} and was rebuilt as many times as well, although finally some of the important buildings, such as one of the three golden halls, the Nandaimon, Chūmon and the corridor were never reconstructed and are missing today. The rebuilding of the Central Golden Hall was completed in 2018. {{nihongo|''[[Rokusō-an]]''|六窓庵|Six Window Hut}} was a ''[[chashitsu]]'' formerly located at the temple and considered one of the {{nihongo|''San-meiseki''|三名席| Three Famous Tearooms}}.{{Google books|MorTAgAAQBAJ|Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac|page=163|keywords=San-meiseki|text=|plainurl=}} It was relocated due its deteriorated state and is now in the gardens of the [[Tokyo National Museum]].{{Cite web|url=https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/ondemand/audio/j_art-20150618-1/|title = Rokusoan Teahouse (Rokusoan) – the Magic of Japanese Masterpieces | NHK WORLD-JAPAN on Demand}}{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%AD%E7%AA%93%E5%BA%B5-879272|title = 六窓庵とは}} == Architectures and treasures == [[File:Koufuku-ji Nan'endo 201505a.jpg|thumb|220px|Nan'endō is the No.9 of [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku 33 Pilgrimage]]]] [[File:181029 Chū-kondō of Kōfuku-ji.jpg|thumb|220px|Chū-kondō (Central Golden Hall)]] [[File:Kofukuji09s3200.jpg|thumb|220px|Tō-kondō (East Golden Hall)]] [[File:ASURA Kohfukuji.jpg|thumb|200px|[[Asura (Buddhism)]]]] The following are some of the temple's buildings and treasures of note. === Architecture === * {{nihongo|East Golden Hall|東金堂|Tō-kondō}}, 1425, one of the former three golden halls ([[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2462 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} *{{nihongo|Central Golden Hall|中金堂|Chū-kondō}}, 2018, reconstructed, the former temporary Central Golden Hall building (仮金堂) now serves as the temporary Lecture Hall (仮講堂) * {{nihongo|Five-storied pagoda|五重塔|Gojū-no-tō}}, 1426 (National Treasure){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2461 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|Three-storied pagoda|三重塔|Sanjū-no-tō}}, 1185(National Treasure){{cite web|url=https://www.kohfukuji.com/english/e003/|title=Three-storied Pagoda National Treasure - KOHFUKUJI Temple|lang=en|access-date=9 September 2023}} * {{nihongo|North Octagonal Hall|北円堂|Hoku'endō}}, 1210 (National Treasure){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2459 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|South Octagonal Hall|南円堂|Nan'endō}}, 1741, Site No.9 of [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku 33 Pilgrimage]] ([[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2464 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|Bath House|大湯屋|Ōyūya}}, 1394–1427 (Important Cultural Property){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2463 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} === Treasures === * (Statue) The [[Deva (Buddhism)|Devas]] of the Eight Classes, including dry-lacquer [[Asura (Buddhism)|Ashura]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) [[The ten principal disciples|The Ten Great Disciples]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) [[Avalokiteśvara#Manifestations|Thousand-armed Kannon]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) {{nihongo|[[Avalokiteśvara#Manifestations|Amoghapāśa]]|不空羂索観音|Fukūkensaku Kannon}} attributed to [[Kōkei (sculptor)|Kōkei]], is housed in Nan'endō (National Treasure) == Plan == Showing the original layout of the temple, with the later three-storied pagoda, Nan'en-dō, and Ōyūya superimposed. Of the buildings marked, only these three together with the five-storied pagoda, Tōkon-dō and Hoku'en-dō remain.{{cite book |title=Early Buddhist Architecture in Japan |author=Suzuki, Kakichi |publisher=[[Kodansha]] |year=1980 |page=86 |isbn=0-87011-386-0}} {| width="415px" align="center" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0" |- | align=center|

Kōfuku-ji was the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]]'s tutelary temple, and enjoyed prosperity for as long as the family did. The temple was damaged and destroyed by civil wars and fires many times,{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |isbn=1-85409-523-4 |page=200}} and was rebuilt as many times as well, although finally some of the important buildings, such as one of the three golden halls, the Nandaimon, Chūmon and the corridor were never reconstructed and are missing today. {{nihongo|''San-meiseki''|三名席| Three Famous Tearooms}}.{{Google books|MorTAgAAQBAJ|Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac|page=163|keywords=San-meiseki|text=|plainurl=}} It was relocated due its deteriorated state and is now in the gardens of the [[Tokyo National Museum]].{{Cite web|url=https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/ondemand/audio/j_art-20150618-1/|title = Rokusoan Teahouse (Rokusoan) – the Magic of Japanese Masterpieces | NHK WORLD-JAPAN on Demand}}{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%AD%E7%AA%93%E5%BA%B5-879272|title = 六窓庵とは}} == Architectures and treasures == [[File:Koufuku-ji Nan'endo 201505a.jpg|thumb|220px|Nan'endō is the No.9 of [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku 33 Pilgrimage]]]] [[File:181029 Chū-kondō of of Japan|National Treasure]]){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2462 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} *{{nihongo|Central Golden Hall|中金堂|Chū-kondō}}, 2018, reconstructed, the former temporary Central Golden Hall building (仮金堂) now serves as the temporary Lecture Hall (仮講堂) * May 2011}} * {{nihongo|Bath House|大湯屋|Ōyūya}}, 1394–1427 (Important Cultural Property){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2463 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} === Treasures === * (Statue) The [[Deva (Buddhism)|Devas]] of the Eight Classes, including dry-lacquer [[Asura (Buddhism)|Ashura]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) [[The ten principal disciples|The Ten Great Disciples]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) [[Avalokiteśvara#Manifestations|Thousand-armed Kannon]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) {{nihongo|[[Avalokiteśvara#Manifestations|Amoghapāśa]]|不空羂索観音|Fukūkensaku Kannon}} attributed to [[Kōkei (sculptor)|Kōkei]], is housed in Nan'endō (National Treasure) == Of the buildings marked, only these three together with the five-storied pagoda, Tōkon-dō and Hoku'en-dō remain.{{cite book |title=Early Buddhist Architecture in Japan |author=Suzuki, Kakichi |publisher=[[Kodansha]] |year=1980 |page=86 |isbn=0-87011-386-0}} {| width="415px" align="center" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0" |- | align=center|

revision.text:
{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Nara, Japan}} {{About|the World Heritage Site|the shrine in Nagasaki|Kōfuku-ji (Nagasaki)}} {{redirect|Kofuku|the 1981 film|Kofuku (film)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kōfuku-ji | native_name = | image = [[File:Kofukuji12st5s3200.jpg|300px]] | image_size = | alt = | caption = Five-story pagoda and Tōkondō at Kōfuku-ji | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 48 Noboriōji-chō, [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Nara Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34.68325|135.83117|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = [[Hossō]] | deity = [[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyorai]] (Śākyamuni) | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{url|http://www.kohfukuji.com/}} | founded_by = [[Emperor Tenji]] | year_completed = 669 }} {{nihongo|'''Kōfuku-ji'''|興福寺|Kōfuku-ji}} is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[Buddhist temples in Japan|temple]] that was once one of the powerful [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples]] in the city of [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Japan]]. The temple is the national headquarters of the [[Dharma character school|Hossō]] school. == History == [[File:Five story Pagoda of Kohuku-ji (temple) in Nara, Japan (1911 by Elstner Hilton).jpg|left|thumb|Kofuku-ji in 1911]] Kōfuku-ji has its origin as a temple that was established in 669 by Kagami-no-Ōkimi ({{nihongo2|鏡大君}}), the wife of [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]], wishing for her husband's recovery from illness. Its original site was in [[Yamashina-ku, Kyoto|Yamashina]], [[Yamashiro Province]] (present-day [[Kyoto]]). In 672, the temple was moved to [[Fujiwara-kyō]], the first planned Japanese capital to copy the orthogonal grid pattern of [[Chang'an]]. In 710, the temple was dismantled for the second time and moved to its present location, on the east side of the newly constructed capital, [[Heijō-kyō]], today's Nara. Kōfuku-ji was the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]]'s tutelary temple, and enjoyed prosperity for as long as the family did. The temple was not only an important center for the Buddhist religion, but also retained influence over the imperial government, and even by "aggressive means" in some cases.John Bowring, pp.218–219 When many of the [[Nanto Shichi Daiji]], such as [[Tōdai-ji]], declined after the move of capital to Heian-kyō (Kyoto), Kōfuku-ji kept its significance because of its connection to the Fujiwara. The temple was damaged and destroyed by civil wars and fires many times,{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |isbn=1-85409-523-4 |page=200}} and was rebuilt as many times as well, although finally some of the important buildings, such as one of the three golden halls, the Nandaimon, Chūmon and the corridor were never reconstructed and are missing today. The rebuilding of the Central Golden Hall was completed in 2018. {{nihongo|''[[Rokusō-an]]''|六窓庵|Six Window Hut}} was a ''[[chashitsu]]'' formerly located at the temple and considered one of the {{nihongo|''San-meiseki''|三名席| Three Famous Tearooms}}.{{Google books|MorTAgAAQBAJ|Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac|page=163|keywords=San-meiseki|text=|plainurl=}} It was relocated due its deteriorated state and is now in the gardens of the [[Tokyo National Museum]].{{Cite web|url=https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/ondemand/audio/j_art-20150618-1/|title = Rokusoan Teahouse (Rokusoan) – the Magic of Japanese Masterpieces | NHK WORLD-JAPAN on Demand}}{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%AD%E7%AA%93%E5%BA%B5-879272|title = 六窓庵とは}} == Architectures and treasures == [[File:Koufuku-ji Nan'endo 201505a.jpg|thumb|220px|Nan'endō is the No.9 of [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku 33 Pilgrimage]]]] [[File:181029 Chū-kondō of Kōfuku-ji.jpg|thumb|220px|Chū-kondō (Central Golden Hall)]] [[File:Kofukuji09s3200.jpg|thumb|220px|Tō-kondō (East Golden Hall)]] [[File:ASURA Kohfukuji.jpg|thumb|200px|[[Asura (Buddhism)]]]] The following are some of the temple's buildings and treasures of note. === Architecture === * {{nihongo|East Golden Hall|東金堂|Tō-kondō}}, 1425, one of the former three golden halls ([[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2462 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} *{{nihongo|Central Golden Hall|中金堂|Chū-kondō}}, 2018, reconstructed, the former temporary Central Golden Hall building (仮金堂) now serves as the temporary Lecture Hall (仮講堂) * {{nihongo|Five-storied pagoda|五重塔|Gojū-no-tō}}, 1426 (National Treasure){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2461 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|Three-storied pagoda|三重塔|Sanjū-no-tō}}, 1185(National Treasure){{cite web|url=https://www.kohfukuji.com/english/e003/|title=Three-storied Pagoda National Treasure - KOHFUKUJI Temple|lang=en|access-date=9 September 2023}} * {{nihongo|North Octagonal Hall|北円堂|Hoku'endō}}, 1210 (National Treasure){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2459 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|South Octagonal Hall|南円堂|Nan'endō}}, 1741, Site No.9 of [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku 33 Pilgrimage]] ([[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2464 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|Bath House|大湯屋|Ōyūya}}, 1394–1427 (Important Cultural Property){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2463 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} === Treasures === * (Statue) The [[Deva (Buddhism)|Devas]] of the Eight Classes, including dry-lacquer [[Asura (Buddhism)|Ashura]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) [[The ten principal disciples|The Ten Great Disciples]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) [[Avalokiteśvara#Manifestations|Thousand-armed Kannon]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) {{nihongo|[[Avalokiteśvara#Manifestations|Amoghapāśa]]|不空羂索観音|Fukūkensaku Kannon}} attributed to [[Kōkei (sculptor)|Kōkei]], is housed in Nan'endō (National Treasure) == Plan == Showing the original layout of the temple, with the later three-storied pagoda, Nan'en-dō, and Ōyūya superimposed. Of the buildings marked, only these three together with the five-storied pagoda, Tōkon-dō and Hoku'en-dō remain.{{cite book |title=Early Buddhist Architecture in Japan |author=Suzuki, Kakichi |publisher=[[Kodansha]] |year=1980 |page=86 |isbn=0-87011-386-0}} {| width="415px" align="center" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0" |- | align=center|

|access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|South Octagonal Hall|南円堂|Nan'endō}}, 1741, Site No.9 of [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku 33 Pilgrimage]] ([[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2464 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|Bath House|大湯屋|Ōyūya}}, 1394–1427 (Important Cultural Property){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2463 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} === Treasures === * (Statue) The [[Deva (Buddhism)|Devas]] of the Eight Classes, including dry-lacquer [[Asura (Buddhism)|Ashura]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) [[The ten principal disciples|The Ten Great Disciples]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) [[Avalokiteśvara#Manifestations|Thousand-armed Kannon]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) {{nihongo|[[Avalokiteśvara#Manifestations|Amoghapāśa]]|不空羂索観音|Fukūkensaku Kannon}} attributed to [[Kōkei (sculptor)|Kōkei]], is housed in Nan'endō (National Treasure) == Plan == Showing the original layout of the temple, with the later three-storied pagoda, Nan'en-dō, and Ōyūya superimposed. Of the buildings marked, only these three together with the five-storied pagoda, Tōkon-dō and Hoku'en-dō remain.{{cite book |title=Early Buddhist Architecture in Japan |author=Suzuki, Kakichi |publisher=[[Kodansha]] |year=1980 |page=86 |isbn=0-87011-386-0}} {| width="415px" align="center" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0" |- | align=center|

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Nara, Japan}} {{About|the World Heritage Site|the shrine in Nagasaki|Kōfuku-ji (Nagasaki)}} {{redirect|Kofuku|the 1981 film|Kofuku (film)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kōfuku-ji | native_name = | image = [[File:Kofukuji12st5s3200.jpg|300px]] | image_size = | alt = | caption = Five-story pagoda and Tōkondō at Kōfuku-ji | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 48 Noboriōji-chō, [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Nara Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34.68325|135.83117|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = [[Hossō]] | deity = [[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyorai]] (Śākyamuni) | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{url|http://www.kohfukuji.com/}} | founded_by = [[Emperor Tenji]] | year_completed = 669 }} {{nihongo|'''Kōfuku-ji'''|興福寺|Kōfuku-ji}} is a [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[Buddhist temples in Japan|temple]] that was once one of the powerful [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples]] in the city of [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Japan]]. The temple is the national headquarters of the [[Dharma character school|Hossō]] school. == History == [[File:Five story Pagoda of Kohuku-ji (temple) in Nara, Japan (1911 by Elstner Hilton).jpg|left|thumb|Kofuku-ji in 1911]] Kōfuku-ji has its origin as a temple that was established in 669 by Kagami-no-Ōkimi ({{nihongo2|鏡大君}}), the wife of [[Fujiwara no Kamatari]], wishing for her husband's recovery from illness. Its original site was in [[Yamashina-ku, Kyoto|Yamashina]], [[Yamashiro Province]] (present-day [[Kyoto]]). In 672, the temple was moved to [[Fujiwara-kyō]], the first planned Japanese capital to copy the orthogonal grid pattern of [[Chang'an]]. In 710, the temple was dismantled for the second time and moved to its present location, on the east side of the newly constructed capital, [[Heijō-kyō]], today's Nara. Kōfuku-ji was the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]]'s tutelary temple, and enjoyed prosperity for as long as the family did. The temple was not only an important center for the Buddhist religion, but also retained influence over the imperial government, and even by "aggressive means" in some cases.John Bowring, pp.218–219 When many of the [[Nanto Shichi Daiji]], such as [[Tōdai-ji]], declined after the move of capital to Heian-kyō (Kyoto), Kōfuku-ji kept its significance because of its connection to the Fujiwara. The temple was damaged and destroyed by civil wars and fires many times,{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |isbn=1-85409-523-4 |page=200}} and was rebuilt as many times as well, although finally some of the important buildings, such as one of the three golden halls, the Nandaimon, Chūmon and the corridor were never reconstructed and are missing today. The rebuilding of the Central Golden Hall was completed in 2018. {{nihongo|''[[Rokusō-an]]''|六窓庵|Six Window Hut}} was a ''[[chashitsu]]'' formerly located at the temple and considered one of the {{nihongo|''San-meiseki''|三名席| Three Famous Tearooms}}.{{Google books|MorTAgAAQBAJ|Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac|page=163|keywords=San-meiseki|text=|plainurl=}} It was relocated due its deteriorated state and is now in the gardens of the [[Tokyo National Museum]].{{Cite web|url=https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/ondemand/audio/j_art-20150618-1/|title = Rokusoan Teahouse (Rokusoan) – the Magic of Japanese Masterpieces | NHK WORLD-JAPAN on Demand}}{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%AD%E7%AA%93%E5%BA%B5-879272|title = 六窓庵とは}} == Architectures and treasures == [[File:Koufuku-ji Nan'endo 201505a.jpg|thumb|220px|Nan'endō is the No.9 of [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku 33 Pilgrimage]]]] [[File:181029 Chū-kondō of Kōfuku-ji.jpg|thumb|220px|Chū-kondō (Central Golden Hall)]] [[File:Kofukuji09s3200.jpg|thumb|220px|Tō-kondō (East Golden Hall)]] [[File:ASURA Kohfukuji.jpg|thumb|200px|[[Asura (Buddhism)]]]] The following are some of the temple's buildings and treasures of note. === Architecture === * {{nihongo|East Golden Hall|東金堂|Tō-kondō}}, 1425, one of the former three golden halls ([[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]]){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2462 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} *{{nihongo|Central Golden Hall|中金堂|Chū-kondō}}, 2018, reconstructed, the former temporary Central Golden Hall building (仮金堂) now serves as the temporary Lecture Hall (仮講堂) * {{nihongo|Five-storied pagoda|五重塔|Gojū-no-tō}}, 1426 (National Treasure){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2461 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|Three-storied pagoda|三重塔|Sanjū-no-tō}}, 1185(National Treasure){{cite web|url=https://www.kohfukuji.com/english/e003/|title=Three-storied Pagoda National Treasure - KOHFUKUJI Temple|lang=en|access-date=9 September 2023}} * {{nihongo|North Octagonal Hall|北円堂|Hoku'endō}}, 1210 (National Treasure){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2459 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|South Octagonal Hall|南円堂|Nan'endō}}, 1741, Site No.9 of [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku 33 Pilgrimage]] ([[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2464 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|Bath House|大湯屋|Ōyūya}}, 1394–1427 (Important Cultural Property){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2463 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} === Treasures === * (Statue) The [[Deva (Buddhism)|Devas]] of the Eight Classes, including dry-lacquer [[Asura (Buddhism)|Ashura]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) [[The ten principal disciples|The Ten Great Disciples]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) [[Avalokiteśvara#Manifestations|Thousand-armed Kannon]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) {{nihongo|[[Avalokiteśvara#Manifestations|Amoghapāśa]]|不空羂索観音|Fukūkensaku Kannon}} attributed to [[Kōkei (sculptor)|Kōkei]], is housed in Nan'endō (National Treasure) == Plan == Showing the original layout of the temple, with the later three-storied pagoda, Nan'en-dō, and Ōyūya superimposed. Of the buildings marked, only these three together with the five-storied pagoda, Tōkon-dō and Hoku'en-dō remain.{{cite book |title=Early Buddhist Architecture in Japan |author=Suzuki, Kakichi |publisher=[[Kodansha]] |year=1980 |page=86 |isbn=0-87011-386-0}} {| width="415px" align="center" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0" |- | align=center|

{{nihongo|''San-meiseki''|三名席| Three Famous Tearooms}}.{{Google books|MorTAgAAQBAJ|Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac|page=163|keywords=San-meiseki|text=|plainurl=}} It was |access-date=14 May 2011}} *{{nihongo|Central Golden Hall|中金堂|Chū-kondō}}, 2018, reconstructed, the former temporary Central Golden Hall building (仮金堂) now serves as the temporary Lecture Hall (仮講堂) * |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|Three-storied pagoda|三重塔|Sanjū-no-tō}}, 1185(National Treasure){{cite web|url=https://www.kohfukuji.com/english/e003/|title=Three-storied Pagoda National Treasure - KOHFUKUJI Temple|lang=en|access-date=9 September 2023}} * {{nihongo|North Octagonal Hall|北円堂|Hoku'endō}}, |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|South Octagonal Hall|南円堂|Nan'endō}}, 1741, Site No.9 of [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku 33 Pilgrimage]] ([[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2464 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} * {{nihongo|Bath House|大湯屋|Ōyūya}}, 1394–1427 (Important Cultural Property){{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2463 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=14 May 2011}} === Treasures === * (Statue) The [[Deva (Buddhism)|Devas]] of the Eight Classes, including dry-lacquer [[Asura (Buddhism)|Ashura]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) [[The ten principal disciples|The Ten Great Disciples]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) [[Avalokiteśvara#Manifestations|Thousand-armed Kannon]] (National Treasure) * (Statue) {{nihongo|[[Avalokiteśvara#Manifestations|Amoghapāśa]]|不空羂索観音|Fukūkensaku Kannon}} attributed to [[Kōkei (sculptor)|Kōkei]], is housed in Nan'endō (National Treasure) == Of the buildings marked, only these three together with the five-storied pagoda, Tōkon-dō and Hoku'en-dō remain.{{cite book |title=Early Buddhist Architecture in Japan |author=Suzuki, Kakichi |publisher=[[Kodansha]] |year=1980 |page=86 |isbn=0-87011-386-0}} {| width="415px" align="center" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0" |- | align=center|
